steps.title: Numbers 11–100
steps.desc: Dutch numbers follow clear patterns. Above twenty, digits REVERSE: 'eenentwintig' = one-and-twenty.

steps.note: Irregular, just memorize it. Good news: it's identical in German.

Irregular — just memorize it, like English.
'tw' sounds like English 'tv'.
From 13 onward, the pattern kicks in.

steps.title: 🧠 Just Like English
steps.text:
Eleven and twelve are irregular in Dutch, English, AND German:

• 11: elf / eleven / elf
• 12: twaalf / twelve / zwölf

From 13 onward, there's a pattern: [number] + tien.
Just like English: thir-teen, four-teen, fif-teen.

steps.note:
drie → der + tien. The vowel shortens.
Same in English: three → thir-teen.
vier → veer + tien. Again the vowel shifts slightly. Pattern: [modified number] + tien.
vijf + tien, straightforward. No vowel change needed here.

steps.title: 🔢 The Teen Pattern
steps.text:
[number] + tien = teen. Some vowels shift:

• 13: dertien (drie → der)
• 14: veertien (vier → veer)
• 15: vijftien
• 16: zestien
• 17: zeventien
• 18: achttien (acht + tien = achttien, double t!)
• 19: negentien

Compare English: thir-teen, four-teen, fif-teen.
Same system, same Germanic family.

steps.note: The first round number above the teens. From here on, a BIG rule kicks in.

een + en + twintig = one-and-twenty.
Unit BEFORE ten. All 20+ numbers work this way.

steps.title: ✏️ The Trema: ë, ï
steps.text:
When two vowels collide across syllables, Dutch adds dots (a trema):

twee + en = tweeën (not 'tweeen')
drie + en = drieën (not 'drieen')

The dots say: 'new syllable starts here!'

deepDive.title: Where else do you see the trema?
deepDive.text:
In numbers:
• 22 = tweeëntwintig
• 23 = drieëntwintig
• 33 = drieëndertig

In country names:
• België (Belgium)
• Italië (Italy)

Also in some common words:
• ruïne (ruin)
• geïnteresseerd (interested)

The trema prevents Dutch readers from misreading the vowel clusters.

steps.title: ⚠️ The Big Reversal
steps.text:
Dutch says the unit BEFORE the ten:

• 21 = eenentwintig (one-and-twenty)
• 23 = drieëntwintig (three-and-twenty)
• 35 = vijfendertig (five-and-thirty)
• 42 = tweeënveertig (two-and-forty)
• 99 = negenennegentig (nine-and-ninety)

The pattern: [unit] + en + [ten]

English used to do this too: 'four-and-twenty blackbirds baked in a pie.' Dutch just kept doing it!

steps.note: Pattern: [modified number] + tig. Same vowel shifts as the teens: drie → der.
vier → veer + tig. Same vowel shift as veertien. The pattern is consistent.

steps.title: 🔢 The Tens: 50, 60, 70
steps.text:
After veertig, the pattern continues:

• 50 = vijftig (vijf + tig)
• 60 = zestig (zes + tig)
• 70 = zeventig (zeven + tig)

All predictable: [number] + tig.
But watch out for the NEXT one...

steps.note: The odd one out! NOT 'achtig' but 'tachtig'. The only irregular ten. Just memorize this one.
Back to the predictable formula: negen + tig. Only tachtig (80) breaks the rule.

Almost identical across all three Germanic languages.
After honderd, the reversal continues: honderdeenentwintig = 121.

steps.q: Which number is the irregular ten?
steps.hint: NOT 'achtig' — the only ten that breaks the pattern

steps.title: Regular Present Tense
steps.desc: Every verb you've seen changes the same way. Time to name the rule: find the stam (stem), add -t or -en. Done.

steps.title: 🔍 Spot the Pattern
steps.text:
Look at the three verbs you already know side by side:

KOMEN:   ik kom,  jij komt,  wij komen
SPREKEN: ik spreek, jij spreekt, wij spreken
WONEN:   ik woon,  jij woont,  wij wonen

See it?

• ik always gets the SHORT form.
• jij always ADDS -t.
• wij always uses the INFINITIVE.

This is not a coincidence. It's a RULE. Let's name it.

steps.title: The Pattern Revealed
steps.label: Three Verbs Side by Side

rows.en: he / she / it comes / speaks / lives

steps.note:
Three different verbs, same endings every time.
ik = stam, jij/u/hij/zij/het = stam + t, wij/jullie/zij = infinitive.

Remove '-en' from any infinitive → that's the stam.
This one concept unlocks every regular verb.

steps.title: 📐 The Stam+t Rule
steps.text:
Three steps to conjugate ANY regular Dutch verb:

1. Take the infinitive: werken
2. Remove -en → the stam: werk
3. Apply the endings:

• ik = stam → ik werk
• jij/u/hij/zij/het = stam + t → jij werkt
• wij/jullie/zij = infinitive → wij werken

That's it. This is called the 'regular present tense.'
Every regular verb in Dutch follows these three steps.

deepDive.title: Why 'ik kom' but 'ik woon'?
deepDive.text:
When you remove -en, the spelling sometimes changes. There are TWO types:

SHORT-VOWEL VERBS (vowel stays short):
• ko-men → kom (short 'o', just remove -en)
• wer-ken → werk (short 'e', just remove -en)

LONG-VOWEL VERBS (vowel must stay long):
• wo-nen → woon (long 'o', so you DOUBLE it)
• le-ren → leer (long 'e', so you DOUBLE it)

Why? In 'wo-nen', the open syllable keeps the 'o' long with one letter. But 'won' is a closed syllable — one 'o' would sound short. So Dutch doubles it: 'woon'.

In 'ko-men', the 'o' is naturally short. 'Kom' in a closed syllable is already correct.

The Foundations spelling rules explain exactly when to double and when not to.

steps.note: Stam = werk. A clean regular verb. The 'w' sounds like English 'v'. You'll use this one every day.
Stam alone, no ending. 'Werk' has a short 'e' in a closed syllable — no spelling surprises.
Stam + t. Same rule as 'hij komt', 'hij spreekt', 'hij woont'.

steps.title: Werken: to work
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Stam = werk. ik werk, jij werkt, wij werken.
Same pattern as komen, spreken, wonen!

steps.also: 'Leren' also means 'to teach': 'Zij leert ons Nederlands.' = She teaches us Dutch.
steps.note:
Stam = leer. Long-vowel verb: 'ee' doubles.
You are doing this right now!
steps.deepDive:
le-ren → leer.
Open syllable (le-) became closed (leer).
To keep the long vowel → double: 'ee'.
Same pattern as spre-ken → spreek, wo-nen → woon.

steps.note: Stam + t = leert. Same rule, always.

steps.title: 🔑 Three Steps, Any Verb
steps.text:
ALL regular verbs follow the same rule:

1. Find the infinitive (e.g. werken)
2. Remove -en → stam (werk)
3. ik = stam, jij = stam+t, wij = stam+en

That's it. Works for every regular verb.

deepDive.title: Your five verbs so far
deepDive.text:
• komen → stam: kom → ik kom, jij komt, wij komen
• spreken → stam: spreek → ik spreek, jij spreekt, wij spreken
• wonen → stam: woon → ik woon, jij woont, wij wonen
• werken → stam: werk → ik werk, jij werkt, wij werken
• leren → stam: leer → ik leer, jij leert, wij leren

From now on, every new regular verb you meet, just find the stam and apply the rule.

steps.title: Hoe laat is het?
steps.desc: Telling time in Dutch starts simple: number + 'uur'. But Dutch tells time BACKWARDS from English.
goals: Ask and answer 'What time is it?'

steps.note:
Literally: 'how late'.
Dutch asks 'how late' instead of 'what time'.

'Het' word. Also means 'hour'.
Drie uur = three o'clock OR three hours.

Formula: het is + number + uur.
Simple as that for whole hours!

steps.title: 🕐 Whole Hours
steps.text:
het is één uur = 1:00
het is twee uur = 2:00
het is drie uur = 3:00
het is vier uur = 4:00
...
het is twaalf uur = 12:00

Notice: 'één' gets the accent marks to distinguish it from 'een' (a/an).

steps.note:
'Om' = at (for times).
'Om hoe laat?' = At what time?

The 's = contraction of 'des' (old genitive).
You'll see this pattern in all time-of-day words.

steps.exampleEn: At three o'clock in the afternoon.
steps.note:
Same 's pattern.
Middag = midday/afternoon.

steps.exampleEn: At eight o'clock in the evening.
steps.note:
Same 's pattern.
Avond = evening.

steps.title: 🌅 Time of Day
steps.text:
Dutch uses these time markers:

's ochtends = in the morning (6-12)
's middags = in the afternoon (12-18)
's avonds = in the evening (18-24)
's nachts = at night (0-6)

They go AFTER the time:
Om acht uur 's ochtends = At 8 in the morning.

steps.hint: I work at 8 in the morning

steps.title: Half & Kwart
steps.desc: This is where Dutch time gets WEIRD. 'Half drie' does NOT mean 3:30. It means 2:30. Yes, really. Plus: kwart over and kwart voor.
goals: Tell quarter past and quarter to
Convert between Dutch and English time

steps.note:
'Over' = past/after.
Minutes OVER the hour.

'Voor' = to/before.
Minutes BEFORE the hour.

kwart over = quarter past.
kwart voor = quarter to.

steps.title: ⏰ Over & Voor
steps.text:
Minutes PAST the hour = over:
het is vijf over drie = 3:05
het is tien over drie = 3:10
het is kwart over drie = 3:15

Minutes TO the hour = voor:
het is tien voor vier = 3:50
het is kwart voor vier = 3:45

So far, same as English. Now the trap...

steps.note:
⚠️ BIGGEST TRAP!
'Half drie' = 2:30, NOT 3:30!
Dutch counts TOWARD the next hour.

steps.title: ⚠️ The Half Trap
steps.text:
In English: 'half past two' = 2:30 (looking BACK)
In Dutch: 'half drie' = 2:30 (looking FORWARD to 3)
In German: 'halb drei' = 2:30 (same as Dutch!)

half twee = 1:30
half drie = 2:30
half vier = 3:30
half vijf = 4:30

Rule: half + NUMBER = (NUMBER minus 1):30

This WILL confuse you. It confuses everyone. Drill it!

deepDive.title: Why does Dutch count forward?
deepDive.text:
Dutch (and German) think of 'half' as 'halfway TO' the next hour.

half drie = halfway to three = 2:30

English thinks of 'half past' as 'half an hour PAST' the current hour.

half past two = two plus half an hour = 2:30

Same time, opposite mental model. Dutch looks forward, English looks back. This is the single biggest time-telling mistake for English speakers.

steps.title: 🕐 The Full Clock
steps.text:
3:00 = drie uur
3:15 = kwart over drie
3:30 = half vier (NOT half drie!)
3:45 = kwart voor vier

Key: 3:20–3:40 all reference 'half vier'.

deepDive.title: Full 5-minute chart
deepDive.text:
3:00 = drie uur
3:05 = vijf over drie
3:10 = tien over drie
3:15 = kwart over drie
3:20 = tien voor half vier
3:25 = vijf voor half vier
3:30 = half vier
3:35 = vijf over half vier
3:40 = tien over half vier
3:45 = kwart voor vier
3:50 = tien voor vier
3:55 = vijf voor vier
4:00 = vier uur

steps.hint: Count FORWARD to the next hour

steps.title: Dagen van de Week
steps.desc: Seven days, all ending in '-dag'. Dutch does NOT capitalize them. Plus: 'op' for 'on' with days.

steps.note:
Same word as the greeting 'dag'!
Plural: dagen.

Van + daag = from + day.
Literally: 'from this day'.

Maan + dag = moon + day.
Same as English! NOT capitalized.

Named after the god Tyr (Tiw's day).
Not capitalized in Dutch.

steps.example: Woensdag is de helft van de week.
steps.exampleEn: Wednesday is the middle of the week.
steps.note:
Named after Wodan/Odin.
English: Woden's day → Wednesday.

Donder = thunder. Thor's day!
German: Donnerstag = thunder-day.

Named after goddess Freya/Frigga.
English: Friday = Freya's day.

From Latin 'Saturni dies'.
Same as English Saturday.

Zon + dag = sun + day.
Identical to English!

steps.title: 📅 All Seven Days
steps.text:
maandag = Monday (moon-day)
dinsdag = Tuesday (Tyr's day)
woensdag = Wednesday (Woden's day)
donderdag = Thursday (thunder-day)
vrijdag = Friday (Freya's day)
zaterdag = Saturday (Saturn's day)
zondag = Sunday (sun-day)

⚠️ NEVER capitalized in Dutch!
'Op' + day = on that day: 'Op maandag werk ik.'

steps.note:
'Op' + day = on that day.
'Op maandag', 'op dinsdag', etc.

steps.example: In het weekend werk ik niet.
steps.exampleEn: On the weekend I don't work.
steps.note:
Het-word. Borrowed from English!
'In het weekend' uses 'in', not 'op'.

steps.exampleEn: What do you do on Monday?
steps.note:
Your sixth verb! 'oe' is ONE sound (like 'oo' in 'food').
Stam = doe (not 'do'). You can't split 'oe'.

Stam = doe. Why not 'do'?
'oe' is a single vowel sound. doen = doe + n, not do + en.
Stam + t = doet. Same ending as hij werkt, hij komt.

steps.title: Doen: to do
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Stam = doe (not 'do', because 'oe' is one sound).
ik doe, jij doet, wij doen. Endings: same as every verb.

steps.title: 📅 Wat doe je op...?
steps.text:
Now you can talk about your week!

Wat doe je op maandag? = What do you do on Monday?
Ik werk op maandag. = I work on Monday.
Wat doe je op vrijdag? = What do you do on Friday?
Ik leer Nederlands. = I learn Dutch.

deepDive.title: Why 'doe' and not 'doet'?
deepDive.text:
Normal: Jij doet het. = You do it. (stam + t)
Question: Wat doe je? = What do you do? (no -t!)

When 'jij/je' comes AFTER the verb, the -t drops:
jij doet → doe jij?
jij werkt → werk jij?
jij komt → kom jij?

This applies to ALL verbs, not just doen.
You'll learn the full rule later.

steps.q: Dutch days of the week are:
opts: capitalized at start of sentence only

steps.s: Op {1} werk ik. Op {2} rust ik.

steps.hint: 'oe' is one sound, you can't split it

steps.s: Op maandag {1} ik. Wat {2} je op dinsdag?
steps.hint: ik = stam. Question with je = stam

steps.title: Maanden en Seizoenen
steps.desc: Good news: Dutch months look almost identical to English. Bad news: they're NOT capitalized (like days). Plus: four seasons to complete your calendar vocabulary.

steps.note:
De-word. Plural: maanden.
Final -d sounds like -t (devoicing).

Almost identical to English.
NOT capitalized in Dutch!

Same as English, minus one letter.
Not capitalized.
Long 'aa'. Compare: March / März (German).

steps.exampleEn: April does what it wants. (Dutch proverb!)
steps.note:
Identical to English.
The proverb means: April weather is unpredictable.
Shortest Dutch month name. Easy cognate.

steps.title: 🗓️ All 12 Months
steps.text:
januari = January
februari = February
maart = March
april = April
mei = May
juni = June
juli = July
augustus = August
september = September
oktober = October
november = November
december = December

⚠️ NEVER capitalized in Dutch!
'In' + month: 'In januari is het koud.'

steps.example: In de winter is het koud.
steps.exampleEn: In the winter it is cold.
steps.note:
'Het is koud' = it is cold.
Final -d sounds like -t (devoicing again).

steps.example: In de zomer is het warm.
steps.exampleEn: In the summer it is warm.
steps.note:
Identical to English!
W = V sound: 'varm'.

steps.example: In de lente bloeien de bloemen.
steps.note:
De-word. Also: 'het voorjaar' (before-year).
Maart, april, mei.

De-word. Juni, juli, augustus.
Compare: summer / Sommer / zomer.

steps.example: In de herfst vallen de bladeren.
steps.note:
De-word. September, oktober, november.
Related to English 'harvest'!

De-word. December, januari, februari.
Identical to English!

steps.title: 🌸 Seasons + Months
steps.text:
In de lente: maart, april, mei
In de zomer: juni, juli, augustus
In de herfst: september, oktober, november
In de winter: december, januari, februari

'In de' + season: In de zomer is het warm.
'In' + month: In januari is het koud.

steps.q: Which month looks almost identical in English and Dutch?

steps.s: In {1} is het warm. In {2} vallen de bladeren.

steps.title: Altijd, Soms, Nooit
steps.desc: How often do you do things? Dutch frequency adverbs go AFTER the verb, not before it like English.
goals: Where to place them in a sentence

steps.note:
Literally 'all-time' (al + tijd).
Goes AFTER the verb: Ik drink altijd koffie.

steps.example: Zij fietst vaak naar het werk.
steps.note:
Very common word. Same position as altijd:
AFTER the verb: 'Zij fietst vaak...'

steps.example: We eten meestal om zes uur.
steps.exampleEn: We usually eat at six o'clock.
steps.note:
Meest + al = most + all.
Slightly more formal than 'vaak'.

Can go BEFORE the subject too!
'Soms werk ik...' = 'Sometimes I work...'
When soms starts the sentence, the verb stays second.

English 'seldom' is the same word!
Not super common in casual Dutch —
many people say 'bijna nooit' (almost never) instead.

Strong word. The Dutch use it directly:
'Dat doe ik nooit.' = I never do that.
No double negative needed (unlike French/Spanish).

steps.title: 📍 Where Do They Go?
steps.text:
English: adverb BEFORE the verb → I always drink coffee.
Dutch: adverb AFTER the verb → Ik drink altijd koffie.

Pattern: SUBJECT + VERB + adverb + rest

⚠️ soms/meestal can START the sentence (V2 rule applies):
Soms werk ik op zaterdag.

deepDive.title: The V2 rule in action
deepDive.text:
Dutch is a 'verb-second' language.
The conjugated verb is ALWAYS in position 2.

Normal: Ik drink altijd koffie.
  1=ik, 2=drink, 3=altijd, 4=koffie

Fronted adverb: Soms drink ik koffie.
  1=soms, 2=drink, 3=ik, 4=koffie

The verb didn't move — 'soms' took position 1,
so 'ik' had to move to position 3.

This is the same rule you already know from:
Op maandag werk ik. (On Monday I work.)
Vandaag leer ik Nederlands. (Today I learn Dutch.)

Anything can go in position 1, but the verb stays in position 2. Always.

steps.note:
'Te' here means 'too'.
'Te laat' = too late. 'Te vroeg' = too early.
The Dutch value punctuality!

Long 'oe' sound (like 'oo' in 'food').
Opposite of 'laat'.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Dutch & Punctuality
steps.text:
The Dutch are famously punctual.

'Op tijd' = on time.
'Te laat' = too late.

'Ik kom nooit te laat.' = something the Dutch say with pride.

deepDive.title: How punctual, exactly?
deepDive.text:
If a meeting is at 14:00, arrive at 13:55.
If dinner is at 18:00, don't show up at 18:30.

'Hij is altijd te laat.' is a genuine complaint in Dutch culture.

Fun fact: Dutch trains report delays in increments of 1 minute.
'De trein is 3 minuten te laat.' = The train is 3 minutes late.

Punctuality runs deep in Dutch society — it's considered basic respect.

steps.q: 'I always drink coffee' in Dutch word order is:

steps.hint: Verb in position 2, adverb after it

steps.s: Wij eten {1} om zes uur. Hij komt {2} te laat.

steps.q: 'Soms werk ik op zaterdag.' Why is 'ik' after the verb?
opts: It's wrong, should be 'Soms ik werk'

steps.s: ___ eet ik vlees. (= I rarely eat meat.)

steps.q: The Dutch word for 'seldom' looks like its English cousin:

steps.s: {1} leer ik Nederlands. Ik werk {2} op zondag.
steps.hint: Sometimes I learn... I never work on...

steps.title: Unit 3: Alles Samen
steps.desc: Numbers, time, days, months, seasons, frequency adverbs — and the regular present tense. No new words. Everything from L1–L7 mixed together.
goals: Tell the time (hele uur, half, kwart)

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After Unit 3 you can:

• Count from 1 to 100
• Tell the time — including the tricky 'half'
• Name all 7 days (not capitalized!)
• Name all 12 months and 4 seasons
• Say how often you do things
• Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense
• Use V2 word order with fronted adverbs

That's a full calendar life in Dutch. Let's test it.

steps.hint: Half TO five, not half past

steps.q: In Dutch, days and months are:

steps.s: Ik drink ___ koffie. (= I always drink coffee.)

steps.hint: Adverb after verb in normal word order

steps.s: Op {1} werk ik. In {2} is het koud.

steps.hint: The -t drops when jij follows the verb

steps.q: 'Soms werk ik op zaterdag.' The verb is in position:

steps.s: Het is {1} voor acht. Ik werk {2} op vrijdag.

steps.hint: Usually we eat at six — adverb first, verb second
